content.extracted_text:
Vat et
i,
q 45. [Fine Bindings] STRACHEY, ELIZABETH. Translations from the MES
French of Emile Souvestre. N.p. (Privately Printed), 1856.
$1250.00
Two vols., sm. 4to., full dark blue ctushed-morocco over
covers heavily ornamented with gilt and blind relief decoration, far too elaborate to
describe. At the top and bottom (on the edges of the outer gilt fillet borders) are
two gilt lines: TOQ*THE*MEMORY*OF*ELIZABETH STRACHEY* and « NON «
ANIMUM:. Board edges gilt, inner gilt dentelles, a.e.g. On the inside rear paste-down
of the first volume is the binder’s ticket of Leighton Son & On the front fly
leaf of volume one is the inscription, “Constance Strachey from E. S. 1 June 1887.”
Elizabeth Strachey was born 14 February. 1810, the eldest daughter of R. W.
| Wilkinson of Hall, Bedfordshire. She married Edward Strachey of
Sutton Court, Pensford, Somerset on 27 August 1844 and died without issue on 11] et
ASE April 1855. Emile Souvestre (1806-1854) was a French playwright who did
much better with his novels than for the stage, although he deliberate y aimed at
making the novel an engine of moral instruction. He is said to have harmo- a hs
ny with Mrs. Strachey’s translations of his works, which include The Bargeman of
the Loire, The Lazaretto-Keeper, Confessions of a Working Man, The Sea-Shore
Gleaner, An Attic Philosopher in Paris, Family Memoirs and The Widower’s Old ON
Age (three chapters of which Edward Strachey added to Vol. IT from M. Souvestre’s
unfinished work). These volumes were published: “Jo the memory of her to whom
these translations are chiefly due, while her hand, in them, as in all t ings else, was ever
joined with that of her husband.”
vA The bindings by Leighton could not be more elaborate and were certainly done
expressly for these privately printed volumes. We know of one other copy of the
work in identical bindings in a private London collection (there are no copies in
either The British or NUC). The brass stamping plates were used again by
| Leighton for an 1868 edition of Oliver Goldsmith's The Traveler in a somewhat
j altered fashion (i.e. he removed the memorial lines at the top and bottom, replac- a
ing two lines of gilt decoration to try and achieve balance; he also ORE
4 removed Elizabeth Strachey’s initials from the central panel on the covers, replacing -
it with an uninspired design element). These beautiful and rare bindings are in FINE A
| CONDITION,

audit_trail.name: Greyscale Conversion
audit_trail.description: Collapsed BGR to single-channel greyscale. Colour is irrelevant for OCR.

audit_trail.name: Bilateral Denoising Filter
audit_trail.description: Edge-aware bilateral filter (d=9, sigmaColor=75, sigmaSpace=75). Removes paper grain and scanner noise while preserving sharp ink-stroke boundaries that OCR depends on.

audit_trail.name: CLAHE Contrast Enhancement
audit_trail.description: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalisation (clipLimit=2.0, tile=8x8). Recovers faded ink strokes and compensates for uneven lighting across the document surface.

audit_trail.name: Adaptive Gaussian Binarization
audit_trail.description: Local Gaussian-weighted threshold per 15x15 neighbourhood (C=10). Isolates ink strokes from yellow staining and background gradients that would defeat global thresholding.

audit_trail.name: OCR — Tesseract LSTM (PSM 6, OEM 3)
audit_trail.description: 72% mean word confidence. 67% of tokens high-confidence (>=80%). Tokens below 40% dropped. Noise lines stripped from top/bottom of output.

audit_trail.name: Annotated Image
audit_trail.description: Per-word bounding boxes: green >=80%, yellow 50-79%, red <50%.

audit_trail.name: Layout Map
audit_trail.description: Morphological dilation (kernel 15x5, 2 iterations) merged characters into paragraph blobs.

ai_analysis.artifact_profile:
Physical Format
Materiality: [Two volumes, small 4to; dark blue crushed-morocco over covers; gilt and blind relief decoration; brass stamping plates]
Condition: [Fine condition; described as beautiful and rare]
Legibility Score (1-5): [5]

Temporal Origin: [1856]
Geographic Origin: [N.p. (Privately Printed); mentions Bedfordshire and Somerset, England]
Author: [Elizabeth Strachey (Translator); Emile Souvestre (Original Author)]
Role: [Translator and Playwright]
Document Genre
ai_analysis.anthropological_matrix:
Positionality
Intended Audience
Dominant Power Dynamic: [Gendered literary collaboration; the text notes the translator's work was "joined with that of her husband" and he added chapters to her volume.]
Notable Silences: [The voices of the working-class subjects mentioned in the book titles (e.g., "The Bargeman," "Working Man") are absent, as the text focuses on the elite owners and the physical luxury of the binding.]
ai_analysis.taxonomy:
Locations: [Hall, Bedfordshire; Sutton Court, Pensford, Somerset; London; Paris]
Key Figures: [Elizabeth Strachey, Emile Souvestre, Edward Strachey, R. W. Wilkinson, Constance Strachey, Leighton (binder)]
Thematic Tags
Indigenous Lexicon: [None]
ai_analysis.validity_bias:
Proximity
Bias Level (1-5): [3] (The text is a descriptive catalog entry using laudatory language such as "beautiful," "rare," and "too elaborate to describe.")
Primary Value
ai_analysis.executive_summary:
Elevator Pitch: A bibliographic description of a rare, privately printed 1856 two-volume set of Emile Souvestre's works, translated by Elizabeth Strachey. The volumes are characterized by highly elaborate, custom-made dark blue crushed-morocco bindings by Leighton.
Key Takeaway: The document illustrates 19th-century elite patterns of private book production, luxury craftsmanship, and gendered literary collaboration.
Next Steps: Investigate the Leighton binding workshop's output and the social/literary connections between the Strachey and Wilkinson families.
